figures.gloss: The Ewe supreme creator dyad. Mawu is associated with the female, the moon, cooling, and gentleness, while Lisa is the male, the sun, heat, and strength. Together they form the self-contained, unapproachable universal architect.
figures.facet: Creation is not enacted by a solitary paternal sovereign but by a perfectly balanced, co-equal hermaphroditic or twin unity, inextricably linking existence to gender complementarity.
figures.provenance: Judy Rosenthal, 'Possession, Ecstasy, and Law in Ewe Voodoo' (1998); Jakob Spieth, 'Die Ewe-Stämme' (1906).

figures.gloss: The Ga supreme being, whose name literally translates to 'Grandfather-Grandmother God' or 'Father-Mother Rain'. Operating above all lesser spirits, this entity is the ultimate source of life, weather, and the cosmos.
figures.facet: The linguistic fusion of male (Ataa) and female (Naa) titles directly into the sky god's name ensures the supreme being transcends biological sex while encompassing the totality of generation.
figures.provenance: Margaret Field, 'Religion and Medicine of the Gã People' (1937); Marion Kilson, 'Kpele Lala: Ga Religious Songs and Symbols' (1971).

figures.gloss: The Ewe system of geomantic divination and the deity of wisdom that governs it. Through the casting of sacred palm nuts or chains, it yields mathematical codes that reveal a person's destiny and the demands of the unseen world.
figures.facet: Afa operates as the essential translator between the remote creator, the spirits, and humanity; without its binary code, the universe's intentions and the cause of suffering remain dangerously illegible.
figures.provenance: Judy Rosenthal, 'Possession, Ecstasy, and Law in Ewe Voodoo' (1998); G.K. Nukunya, 'Kinship and Marriage Among the Anlo Ewe' (1969).

figures.gloss: The Ewe pantheon of active spiritual forces and territorial deities. Residing in rivers, trees, and stones, they mediate between humans and Mawu-Lisa, demanding regular propitiation to ensure communal well-being and fertility.
figures.facet: They are not merely nature spirits but highly localized legal and moral enforcers, frequently possessing their human hosts to adjudicate disputes, expose witchcraft, and demand justice in village affairs.
figures.provenance: Judy Rosenthal, 'Possession, Ecstasy, and Law in Ewe Voodoo' (1998).

figures.gloss: The Ga deities of nature and the world, subordinate only to Ataa-Naa Nyongmo. They control the sea, the coastal lagoons, and the harvest, and are intimately tied to specific Ga lineages and their priestly Wulomei.
figures.facet: Their jurisdictions are intensely geographic—tied specifically to the lagoons and coastlines of the Accra plains—making Ga religious practice fundamentally rooted in local topography and lineage descent.
figures.provenance: Margaret Field, 'Religion and Medicine of the Gã People' (1937).

figures.gloss: The paramount Ga festival, meaning 'hooting at hunger'. It commemorates the ancestral survival of a severe historical famine through the ritual scattering of sacred kpokpoi food and the lifting of a ban on drumming.
figures.facet: It is not merely a passive agricultural thanksgiving but an aggressive, triumphant mocking of starvation itself, turning collective historical trauma into an annual reinvigoration of the social order.
figures.provenance: Marion Kilson, 'Kpele Lala' (1971); Irene Odotei, 'The History of the Ga People' (2006).

figures.gloss: The Ewe guardian of crossroads and thresholds, intimately connected with Afa divination. He is the volatile, phallic trickster who introduces contingency, accident, and choice into human destiny.
figures.facet: He is both the cause of disruption and its only cure. He must be fed first in every ritual because he holds the key to the gates of communication; without his cooperation, the gods hear nothing.
figures.provenance: Judy Rosenthal, 'Possession, Ecstasy, and Law in Ewe Voodoo' (1998).

figures.gloss: The Ewe concept of the personal soul or destiny, breathed into a person by Mawu before birth. It dictates one's lot in life, which can only be decoded and navigated through Afa divination.
figures.facet: Destiny here is not a rigid, inescapable fatalism but a negotiated contract; through divination and sacrifice, one can realign with their 'Se' to mitigate misfortune and optimize their path.
figures.provenance: G.K. Nukunya, 'Kinship and Marriage Among the Anlo Ewe' (1969).

edges.note: Intra-tradition bond: The remote, ultimate creator dyad and the active, localized spiritual enforcers who execute their will on earth.
Intra-tradition bond: The objective, mathematical truth of destiny (Afa) bound inextricably to the volatile, contingent trickster (Legba) who delivers it.
Intra-tradition bond: The overarching sky-parent and the terrestrial, coastal deities who govern the lived environment and economy.
Intra-tradition bond: The coastal gods and the paramount agricultural festival where they are fed, thanked, and renewed.
Intra-tradition bond: The personal destiny allotted before birth and the divinatory mechanism required to read and negotiate it.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Direct regional cognate and shared theological complex across the neighboring Gbe-speaking peoples.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The identical crossroads trickster and linguist figure shared across Ewe and Fon traditions.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The shared geomantic divination system derived from the broader regional oracle complex.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Historical and functional continuity of the phallic, crossroads trickster and divine messenger.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A supreme creator entity that is fundamentally a male-female dyad in perfect equilibrium.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The cosmos understood as an interplay of complementary male/sun/strength and female/moon/gentleness principles.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A neighboring supreme sky-god parallel, functionally similar in governing rain and the ultimate life force.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Active, localized deities inhabiting natural features, mediating between humans and a supreme creator.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A highly formalized, codified system for reading the intentions of the gods to navigate destiny and avert disaster.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The divine messenger and lord of the crossroads who bridges the gap between the sacred and profane.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A fragment of the divine or creator's essence lodged within the human, dictating personal truth and trajectory.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A massive, cyclical communal festival that commemorates ancestral history and resets the social-spiritual clock.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Localized nature spirits deeply tied to territorial boundaries and water sources.

dossier.essence: The traditional religions of two neighboring West African peoples: the Ewe of southeastern Ghana and Togo (Gbe-speaking, and closely cognate with the Fon Vodun of Benin) and the Ga of the Accra plains in Ghana (Kwa-speaking). Both recognize a remote supreme creator above a populous world of active deities and ancestral spirits, and both remain living traditions alongside Christianity and Islam.
dossier.cosmos: The Ewe name the creator as the dual Mawu-Lisa (or Mawu alone) and the Ga as Ataa-Naa Nyongmo ('Father-Mother God'), both fusing male and female in the highest being; beneath the creator stand the Ewe trowo (deities of river, sea, thunder, and place) and the Ga dzemawoji (gods of sea and lagoon), tied to particular lineages and locales. A person carries a destiny (Ewe Se/Dzogbese) allotted before birth, and the moral order is made legible through divination. The dead become ancestors who remain involved in the affairs of their descendants.
dossier.practice: Worship runs through lineage- and shrine-based priesthoods (the Ga wulomei), through spirit-possession in which deities 'mount' and speak through devotees to judge and heal, through sacrifice and propitiation, and through the geomantic system Afa, read by casting palm nuts or a divining chain; the trickster-linguist Legba must be fed first at every rite. The Ga mark the year with Homowo ('hooting at hunger'), the great festival commemorating survival of a founding famine.
dossier.canon: Oral in transmission — myth, the divination verse of the Afa corpus, praise-song, and festival liturgy — documented in the ethnographies of Margaret Field, Marion Kilson, and Judy Rosenthal rather than in any indigenous scripture.
dossier.reflects: It foregrounds the localization of the divine — a remote, androgynous creator made accessible through place-bound deities, lineage ancestors, and a divination code that renders personal destiny both legible and negotiable.
sources: Judy Rosenthal, 'Possession, Ecstasy, and Law in Ewe Voodoo' (1998)
Margaret J. Field, 'Religion and Medicine of the Ga People' (1937)
Marion Kilson, 'Kpele Lala: Ga Religious Songs and Symbols' (1971)
